title: Key rotation attestation — KMS/HSM rotation SLA tracker
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate key-wrap hierarchy; KEK/DEK design is out of scope.
evidence: Daily report 'kms_rotation_sla' writes per-key rotation evidence to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 3.6 requires key rotation at end-of-cryptoperiod; the UC evidences compliance per key.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 3.6 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.3: Key rotation attestation — KMS/HSM rotation SLA tracker.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.41.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 SC-13 (Cryptographic protection) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.3: Key rotation attestation — KMS/HSM rotation SLA tracker.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.41.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted to full: SPL provides comprehensive, standalone evidence for Art.9 with specific data sources and actionable thresholds.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.9 (Protection and prevention) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.3: Key rotation attestation — KMS/HSM rotation SLA tracker.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.41.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a key's last rotation is older than the cryptoperiod defined in key_policy.csv, the UC fires with key_id, last_rotation, and cryptoperiod.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Keys within their cryptoperiod and keys marked key_type=archive are NOT flagged.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=kms (sourcetype=aws:kms OR sourcetype=azure:keyvault OR sourcetype=gcp:kms) action=rotate
| stats max(_time) as last_rotation by key_id tenant
| lookup key_policy.csv key_id OUTPUT cryptoperiod_days key_type
| where key_type!="archive"
| eval age_days=round((now()-last_rotation)/86400,0)
| where age_days>cryptoperiod_days
| table tenant key_id last_rotation age_days cryptoperiod_days
description: Verifies every encryption key is rotated within its policy cryptoperiod. Overdue keys are highlighted and evidence is written to the compliance index for audit.
value: Gives the cryptographic custodian the ability to defend the 'our keys are rotated' claim with per-key evidence rather than a policy document.
implementation: (1) Ingest KMS audit logs; (2) maintain key_policy.csv; (3) run daily; (4) escalate via SOAR to the crypto custodian. Indexes required: `index=kms`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=aws:kms`, `sourcetype=azure:keyvault`, `sourcetype=gcp:kms`.
visualization: Table of overdue keys, scatter plot age vs cryptoperiod, single-value '% of keys within cryptoperiod'. Key fields: `age_days`, `cryptoperiod_days`, `key_id`, `last_rotation`, `tenant`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised penetration testing** — Annual or more frequent penetration tests conducted by QSA-approved assessors against cardholder data environment (CDE) systems produce expected security alerts that are pre-communicated and tracked via the test engagement letter.

2. **PCI ASV scanning activity** — Quarterly external vulnerability scans performed by Approved Scanning Vendors generate network probe events that trigger IDS/IPS alerts as an expected part of the PCI DSS validation process.

3. **Tokenization system batch processing** — The payment tokenization platform performs scheduled batch re-tokenization and token lifecycle management operations that produce high-volume cardholder data access patterns within approved operational windows.

4. **Acquirer and processor settlement activity** — End-of-day settlement reconciliation between the merchant platform and payment processor involves legitimate PAN/SAD data transmission that is covered by the processor service provider agreement.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `pci_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved pen-test engagement IDs, ASV scan source IPs, scheduled batch job names, and settlement processing windows. The search filters matches where `pci_exception_valid=true` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase app ID 1876) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for AWS" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `kms` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `key_policy.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('key_policy.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `3.6`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI DSS 3.6 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.3: Key rotation attestation — KMS/HSM rotation SLA tracker." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase app ID 1876), landing events in `kms` under `aws:kms`, `azure:keyvault`, `gcp:kms`. CloudTrail management events average 5000-50000 API calls/account/hour for active workloads (~5-50 GB/account/month). Data events (S3, Lambda) can multiply this 10-100x.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > CloudTrail (SQS-based ingest is strongly preferred over S3 polling). A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[aws_cloudtrail://prod_account]
aws_account = production
aws_region = us-east-1
sqs_queue = splunk-ct-queue
index = aws
sourcetype = aws:cloudtrail
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `eventName, eventSource, userIdentity.arn, sourceIPAddress, requestParameters, responseElements, errorCode, userAgent`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=kms earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest KMS audit logs. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** maintain key_policy.csv. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** run daily. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** escalate via SOAR to the crypto custodian. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Key rotation attestation — KMS/HSM rotation SLA tracker," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of PCI DSS control activity.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `key_policy.csv` — the policy register codifying the approved configuration baseline. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`cryptoperiod_days key_type`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `key_id`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `key_policy.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval key_id = lower(key_id)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the full search range.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.41.3: Key rotation attestation — KMS/HSM rotation SLA tracker]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.41.3,reg=PCI_DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=kms sourcetype=aws:kms earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `age_days`, `cryptoperiod_days`, `key_id`, `last_rotation`, `tenant`. Run:

```spl
index=kms sourcetype=aws:kms earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table age_days, cryptoperiod_days, key_id, last_rotation, tenant
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `key_policy.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup key_policy.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.41.3: PCI DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Overdue Keys (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Scatter Plot Age Vs Cryptoperiod (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Of Keys Within Cryptoperiod' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see encryption coverage and gaps so you can show protections stay current across systems.
